questions.question: When securing a patient to a long backboard, you should secure:
options: The head first, then the torso
The torso first, then the head last
questions.rationale: Secure the torso before the head so the head is never the anchor point; otherwise torso shift loads the neck.

questions.question: Manual in-line stabilization should be maintained:
options: From first contact until the patient is fully secured
Only after the collar is applied
questions.rationale: Continuous manual stabilization is held from first contact until full securing to the device.

questions.question: The long backboard is best understood as:
options: The required transport surface for all patients
Primarily an extrication and transfer tool
questions.rationale: The board is mainly for extrication/transfer; patients can often be transported on a padded stretcher with collar and straps.

questions.question: During a log roll, who directs the team and gives commands?
questions.rationale: The provider maintaining head stabilization coordinates and commands the roll.

questions.question: A key purpose of the log roll, besides moving the patient, is to:
options: Inspect and palpate the posterior for injuries
questions.rationale: The roll allows examination of the posterior (DCAP-BLS, TIC) and placement of the board.

questions.question: A short extrication (vest-type) device is indicated for:
options: A seated, stable patient before vehicle extrication
questions.rationale: The short device provides SMR to seated, stable patients prior to extrication.

questions.question: An advantage of the scoop stretcher is that it:
options: Splits to slide under the patient with minimal movement
questions.rationale: The scoop splits in two to slide under the patient from each side, minimizing movement during transfer.

questions.question: A vacuum mattress is beneficial because it:
options: Conforms to the body and distributes pressure while maintaining alignment
questions.rationale: The vacuum mattress molds to the patient, reducing pressure injury and maintaining neutral alignment comfortably.

questions.question: Emergency Rescue is performed when:
options: There is immediate danger to life (fire, drowning)
The patient has neck pain only
questions.rationale: Emergency Rescue prioritizes life over spine when the scene poses immediate danger.

questions.question: A helmet should be removed when:
options: It fits snugly and the airway is accessible
It prevents airway access/assessment or fits poorly
questions.rationale: Remove a helmet that blocks airway/assessment, fits loosely (allowing head movement), or impairs immobilization.

questions.question: Helmet removal in a trauma patient requires:
questions.rationale: Two rescuers are needed—one stabilizes from below while the other removes the helmet to prevent movement.

questions.question: A well-fitting helmet may be left in place if:
options: The airway is accessible and the head can be securely immobilized
questions.rationale: A snug helmet with an accessible airway and secure head immobilization can be left on, with padding as needed.

questions.question: Before and after packaging a spine-injured patient, you must reassess:
options: Distal pulses, motor function, and sensation (PMS)
questions.rationale: Distal neurovascular status (PMS) is checked before and after any movement or packaging.

questions.question: To maintain neutral alignment in a small child on a board, you should:
options: Pad under the torso to offset the large head
questions.rationale: A child's large head flexes the neck on a flat board; padding under the torso restores neutral alignment.

questions.question: A hazard of leaving a patient on a rigid backboard for a long transport is:
questions.rationale: Prolonged rigid-board time causes pressure injury and can impair breathing—transfer to a padded surface when possible.

questions.question: If neutral in-line position cannot be safely achieved, you should:
options: Stabilize and immobilize the head/neck in the position found
questions.rationale: Do not force movement that causes pain or neuro change—immobilize in the position found.

questions.question: Rapid extrication differs from standard extrication in that it:
options: Uses manual stabilization to move a critical patient quickly
questions.rationale: Rapid extrication trades the short device for speed using manual stabilization in critical or unstable situations.

questions.question: An ill-fitting cervical collar can:
options: Force flexion or extension of the neck
questions.rationale: Wrong collar size can push the neck into flexion or extension; correct sizing maintains neutral alignment.
